coredata.dc:title: Preparation and tribological behavior of a self-assemble copper base carbon quantum dot films
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Enzhu
Yu, Xiuqian
Liu, Shusheng
Su, Enhao
Subedi, Ayush
Zhong, Hua
Hu, Kunhong
coredata.dc:description: A copper-based carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) and its films on the surface of steel discs were prepared successfully by the hydrothermal method and simply immersion into Cu-CQDs solution method, respectively, in order to develop a functional additive and high performance of bearing materials. Thus obtained Cu-CQDs has an average particle size of approximately 10 nm. The physical and chemical properties of the prepared Cu-CQDs and its films were studied using various modern instruments like SEM, RAMAN, XRD, etc. An UMT-2 tribometer was conducted to investigate the tribological behavior of lubricated thin films. Here, the films played an important role in modifying of friction and wear properties of bearing steel discs. The lower friction coefficient and wear rate of film-coated steel discs were achieved lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs. The tribological mechanisms of Cu-CQDs and films were ascribed to the formation of lubrication films including the components of Cu-CQDs and the friction-induced formation of transfer film, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Friction and wear
Copper
Abrasive wear

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Wear resistance and friction reduction properties
Surface morphology analysis of wear scars

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for preparation of Cu-CQDs and films.
HRTEM, FTIR and particle distributions of Cu-CQDs
(a) HRTEM images, size distribution of Cu-CQDs (b) The agglomeration size of Cu-CQDs in water (c) FTIR analysis of Cu-CQDs (d) Raman analysis of Cu-CQDs.
SEM and contact angle analysis of Cu-CQDs film on the surface of the disks (a) pure steel disk (b) 0.5 h immersion (c) 1 h immersion (d) 2 h immersion.
Electronic images of steel disk and Cu-CQDs films (a) SEM images of Cu-CQDs films thickness for different immersion times (b: 0.5 h, c: 1 h and d: 2 h).
Adhesion force tests of different Cu-CQD films.
Load-displacement curves of Cu-CQD films for different immersion times.
Schematical diagram of UMT-2 tribometer and analysis routes for mechanism of tribological tests.
Variation of friction coefficient curves of Cu-CQDs film lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs (a) 10 N (b) 50 N (c) 100 N (d) 150 N.
Variation of friction coefficient curves of Cu-CQDs film lubricated with pure water (a) 10 N (b) 50 N (c) 100 N (d) 150 N.
Variation of wear rates of Cu-CQDs film lubricated with different media.
Variation of chemical valences of wear zones lubricated with the different samples at 50 N for 30 min (a) C1s (b) O1s (c) Cu1s.
Schematic diagram of tribological mechanism of Cu-CQDs and its films (COF is coefficient of friction).
EDS and surface roughness of Cu-CQDs films for different immersion times.
Average modulus and hardness of Cu-CQDs films for different immersion times.
Variation in depth and width of friction traces of steel discs at load 50 N and velocity for 30 min lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution.

head.ce:title: Preparation and tribological behavior of a self-assemble copper base carbon quantum dot films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei, 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei 230601, PR. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A copper-based carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) and its films on the surface of steel discs were prepared successfully by the hydrothermal method and simply immersion into Cu-CQDs solution method, respectively, in order to develop a functional additive and high performance of bearing materials. Thus obtained Cu-CQDs has an average particle size of approximately 10 nm. The physical and chemical properties of the prepared Cu-CQDs and its films were studied using various modern instruments like SEM, RAMAN, XRD, etc. An UMT-2 tribometer was conducted to investigate the tribological behavior of lubricated thin films. Here, the films played an important role in modifying of friction and wear properties of bearing steel discs. The lower friction coefficient and wear rate of film-coated steel discs were achieved lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs. The tribological mechanisms of Cu-CQDs and films were ascribed to the formation of lubrication films including the components of Cu-CQDs and the friction-induced formation of transfer film, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As is well known, friction and wear have become one of the major factors causing material and energy consumption. The friction and wear of mechanical component surfaces incur significant economic losses annually, i.e., approximately 6% of the gross national product of developed nations [1,2]. Lowering friction coefficient and wear rate are of great importance for improving the efficiency and prolonging the service life of the wheel stern bearing balls, bearing rings and bearing-rotor system. Lubrication technology is one of the effective ways to address material wear, improve engine fuel efficiency, reduce greenhouse emissions and extend the life of machinery and equipment [3–5]. The adoption of appropriate lubricants or efficient surface coating materials are the powerful and convenient mean to solve the issues. Nanoparticles with unique physicochemical properties and excellent tribological behaviors become the research hotspot with the development of nanotechnology. They have a broad application prospect as solid lubricants or fluid-lubricant additives and coating materials [6,7].

To date, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is one of typical nano-materials which were also prepared from various resources such as rice husk, citric acid, glycerinum et al. [8–15]. CQDs are well known for their fluorescence, low toxicity, chemical inertia, good biocompatibility [16,17], water solubility, high light resistance, bleaching resistance, and environmental friendliness [18], and superior to other semiconductor materials such as Si and Ge [15,19,20]. It is widely used in fluorescence imaging, biosensing, cell detection, drug carrier, and tribological field [21–26]. The spherical structure and abundant surface-active groups of carbon quantum dots determine that they can be functionalized by surface modification or metal doping [27,28]. It is proved that carbon quantum dots and their hybrid materials as lubricating additives can effectively improve the compatibility and long-term stability with base oils and exhibit excellent lubricating properties. The spherical nanostructure of the carbon quantum dots themselves determines their potential tribological properties. In recent years, several researchers have explored and investigated the tribological properties of carbon quantum dot complexes. Zhu et al. [29]reported that the average friction coefficient and wear diameter of n-CDs/2D Ni-BDC castor oil under ball-on-ball contact decreased by 35.29% and 25%, respectively. The average coefficient of friction and wear rate were reduced by 20% and 12.3% respectively under ball-on-slider contact. Cai et al. [30] fabricated metal nanohybrids (CDs-Mo), which were explored as lubricant additives in PEG base oil, resulting in a 62.90% reduction in friction coefficient and 80.21% reduction in wear. The positively charged steel surface attracts CDs-Mo during the friction process, resulting in a reduced fluorescence of PEG containing 1 wt% CDs-Mo, which collects and transforms into an inhomogeneous carbon-based oil film, avoiding direct contact with the friction substrate. Huang et al. [31] reported that CQDs/CuSx nanocomposites exhibited high chemical activity and stability when used as liquid paraffin additives, enhanced lubricity, and the ability to repair metal wear surfaces. Wang et al. [32]prepared carbon dots (CDs-NTF2) at the end of ionic liquid through pyrolysis and anion exchange reaction, reducing wear rates by 70% and 33%. However, the tribological properties of self-lubricating films formed by water-based metal ion-modified carbon dots have been little studied. In our group, the effects of CQDs and metal base CQDs on the tribological behaviors of PEG had been investigated [33,34]. It is very interesting experimental phenomenon that a novel lubricating film on the bearing steel disc was found when the bearing steel ball was immersed in Cu-CQDs solution. However, the tribological properties of self-lubricating films have not been reported until now. In this paper, the tribological behavior of nano-films was investigated under different immersion times, to lay some foundation for the application of carbon dots in order to develop a functional additive and a novel lubrication coating material to promote the anti-wear and anti-friction of bearing materials extend their service life.

Weighing 9.31 g of glycerol solution (Approximate 95 wt%) was obtained from biodiesel production processing. 2.85 g of choline chloride (Analytical grade) crystals, and 2.72 g of copper chloride dihydrate (Analytical grade) crystals were weighed and transferred into a 50 ml three-necked flask in the ratio of 5:1:1, and closed the flask with a rubber stopper to prevent splashing and water loss during heating. Then the three-mouth flask was placed in an oil bath and heated and stirred from room temperature to 110 °C for about 5 h until the solution was homogeneous and stable. Then the solution was cooled to room temperature to obtain the precursor DES (deep eutectic solvent).

Taking 0.5 g DES and 30 ml deionized water, then which were transferred into a 50 ml beaker, and covered with plastic film to prevent liquid splashing and impurities from entering. The DES was stirred for about 2 h using a small magnet to disperse DES uniformly into the water. The stirred DES aqueous solution was placed in a 50 ml polytetrafluoroethylene lined reaction kettle. The shell was tightened, and then transferred the kettle into an oven under the reaction condition of 220 °C and 24 h. After the sufficient reaction, the temperature of mixture solution decreased down to room temperature. The supernatant was filtered out using a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane and freeze drying to obtain the Cu-CQDs.

A certain amount of Cu-CQDs solution was weighed and added to pure water to configure the solution with a mass concentration of about 0.5 wt%. The Cu-CQDs films were prepared by a simple immersion method. The surface of steel disks was pretreated with an abrasive paper for 10 min, then they were immersed into the Cu-CQDs solution with the immersion times 0.5 h, 1 h, and 2 h, respectively. The detailed preparation process of CQDs and films as shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphology of Cu-CQDs in solution was analyzed by high power transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL–2010) with a FEI Tecnai G20 system operating at 200 kV. The specimen was prepared by drop-casting Cu-CQDs aqueous suspension (0.02 mg/ml) onto the carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at the room temperature. Fig. 2 (a) shows the morphology of Cu-CQDs at a 200 nm scale, which shows that most of Cu-CQDs are uniformly distributed in the solution, with uniform particle size and regular spherical shape. At a magnification of 10 nm, the particle shape is close to a circular sphere with the distinct lattice structure. Using the Nano measurer test software, 300 particles were sampled and the particle size was measured to be between approximately 2.02 and 21.54 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 10 nm.

The particle size distribution of Cu-CQDs in water was determined by Zeta potentiometry (model Zs90) with the conductivity range 0–200 mS/cm and scanning angle range of 0–90 °C. The size of the agglomerated particles of Cu-CQDs in water was about 90 nm, which was found to be significantly higher than the particle size measured by HRTEM, indicating that the particles existed as agglomerated particles in water, but the dispersion was still good as shown in the inset glass bottles in Fig. 2(b). The surface functional groups (mainly –OH at 3300 cm−1 and CO at 1635 cm−1) of Cu-CQDs were characterized by infrared spectroscopy (model NicoletiS50) using the KBr tablet method. The result was shown in Fig. 2(c).

The fluorescence spectrometer (model LS55) was conducted to investigate the variation of visual color of Cu-CQDs in water with 365 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature. It can see the yellow Cu-CQDs solution changed into the green as the inset optical images shown in Fig. 2(b)

Raman spectroscopy (RS, LabRAM-HR resolution = 0.6 cm−1, scanning repeatability = 70.2 cm−1, scanning ranges = 50-4000 cm−1) was conducted to investigate the structure of Cu-CQDs. The Cu-CQDs particles have the D (1350 cm−1) and G (1580 cm−1) peaks and others peaks, which maybe belong to the organic groups including copper as shown in Fig. 2 (d).

Generally, the smaller the wetting angle is, the better the water absorption of the material is, and the thinner the water film thickness is formed. After soaking for 1 h, the surface wetting angle of film is the largest, and the water absorption of the reaction material is poor, so the water film thickness should be relatively large. At the same time, the surface roughness of 0.829 μm is also the largest, indicating that both water film formation and surface roughness are larger than the agglomeration size of Cu-CQDs (0.09 μm) in water. The results also could be used to clarify the nano-size effect of the Cu-CQDs particles during friction process.

Fig. 4 shows SEM images of the cross-sections of the films immersion for different times, which show that the thickness of the films immersed for 0.5 h is 117 μm. The 1 h film was the thickest, about 178 μm, and the 2 h film was 126 μm. These results can explain, to some extent, the better anti-wear and friction-reducing properties of the film formed by the 1 h immersion. Regards to the thickness of films become thinner for immersion 2 h than that of thickness of film for immersion 1 h, it maybe the components of film to be exfoliation during immersion process resulting from some micro-pits on the surface film (2 h film thickness in Fig. 3) which is possible ascribed to be attacked by water [35,36], and also can be clarified indirectly by the surface roughness and contact angle decreased as shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1.

An automatic scratching instrument for coating adhesion (model WS-2005) was performed to measure adhesion force of films on the surface of steel discs with a dynamic load of 1 N/s. Fig. 5 shows the adhesion force of Cu-CQD film on the surface of steel disc. The results indicated that the Cu-CQD film can combine with the base surface of steel disc tightly.

The loads as a function of displacement curves of Cu-CQD films for different immersion times were investigated using a MML Nano indenter (model Agilent G200) using the surface approach velocity of 10 nm/s, and depth limit of 1000 nm. The average modulus and hardness of Cu-CQD films for 1 h is 78.9 and 1.78 GPa, which are higher than those of Cu-CQD films for 2 h as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2 .

Using an UMT-2 reciprocating friction tester to explore tribological behavior of Cu-CQDs and films under the different loads (10, 50, 100, and 150 N), reciprocating speed 10 mm/s, stroke 5 mm, within 30 min. The schematic diagram of the friction pairs of the tester is shown in Fig. 7 . The bearing steel balls (6 mm diameter, GCr15 steel, Chinese standard GB/T18254-2016) used were used as the upper specimens. Steel ball hardness is 60–63 RHC. A GCr15 steel disc with a diameter of 28 mm, a thickness of 2 mm, and a surface roughness of 0.106 μm was used as the lower specimen. Steel disc hardness is 50–60 RHC. The steel balls and discs were chosen base on the bearing steel materials which could be applied in the wheel stern bearing ball, bearing ring and bearing-rotor system et al. [37,38]. The main components of GCr15 steel are carbon, iron, cadmium. Before the friction test, the steel ball and steel disc and fixing frame were ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol for 5 min. All friction tests are carried out three times to reduce the testing error.

The wear morphology of the steel balls and discs was analyzed using a three-dimensional laser scanning microscope. Abrasion diameter, abrasion width, and abrasion area are calculated using a multi-document analyzer. Calculating the wear rate of the discs uses the formula as following [39].
K
o = (S
a
×A)/L×N
where, K
o is the wear rate (mm3/N m), Sa is the wear area (μm2), A is the amplitude (mm), L is the sliding distance (m), and N is the load (N).

SEM/EDS tests the morphology of wear marks and their surface element content. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB, Thermo company) was performed in order to characterize the chemical valence states and relative contents of elements in the wear marks to reveal the friction and wear mechanisms with a monochromatized Al KAa source (1486.6 eV), with a pass energy of 20 eV. Internal calibration used the Ce (C, H) components of the C1s spectra at the binding energy (BE) of 283.7 eV as reference. After subtraction of a nonlinear background, the spectra were deconvoluted using the peak fitting software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear resistance and friction reduction properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 8 and 9
show the variation of the friction coefficient curve of the film material under different loads and lubricating media. From the four graphs in Fig. 8, the friction coefficient will be significantly reduced after adding Cu-CQDs in water as a lubricant, indicating a very good lubrication effect.

After the films are prepared by the soaking method, when pure water is used as the lubricating medium, the friction coefficients of the films are also significantly lower than that of steel disc without film under pure water lubrication. Among the films, the film soaked for 1 h had the largest reduction in friction coefficient as shown in Fig. 9.

When 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs were added in water as a lubricant, friction coefficient of the film was also significantly reduced compared with pure water lubrication (in Fig. 8), where the reduction in friction coefficient was also the largest for 1 h immersion. It can be seen that Cu-CQDs and the formation of Cu-CQDs film effectively protects the interface of the substrate material and plays a good friction reduction effect.

Fig. 10 shows the variation in the wear rate of the film material under different loads and lubricating media. The wear rate of steel disc under a load of 10 N and pure water lubrication was 3.63 × 10−9 mm3/Nm. A wear rate of 4.27 × 10−9 mm3/Nm when 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs is added to pure water as a lubricant. When steel disc was immersed in water with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs for different times to form films of different thicknesses, with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs aqueous solutions as a lubricant, the wear rate of 2 h immersion was reduced the most, with a wear rate of 0.059 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 98.6%, indicating that the film immersed for a long time under low load has a good anti-wear effect. When pure water lubrication, the best anti-wear effect of the film immersed for 1 h was achieved with a wear rate of 1.47 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 59.3%.

At a load of 50 N, the wear rate of the steel disc was 2.97 × 10−9 mm3/Nm under pure water lubrication. When 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs was added to pure water, the wear rate of steel disc decreased to 0.97 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, with a reduction of 66.9%. The film formed by soaking the steel disc for different times, under 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs in pure water lubrication, the film formed by soaking for 2 h had better anti-wear properties, with a reduction of 0.032 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, with a reduction of 98.9%. Once again, the film immersed for a long time under low load has a good anti-wear effect, when pure water lubrication of the three films, the film immersed for 1 h has better anti-wear properties with a wear rate of 0.72 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 75.7%.

The wear rate of steel disc is 2.13 × 10−9 mm3/Nm under a load of 100 N under pure water lubrication. When 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs is added to pure water, the steel disc wear rate will be 0.70 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 67.1%. The film formed by soaking the steel disc for different times, under pure water lubrication, it is interesting to find that the film formed at 1 h of soaking has better anti-wear performance, and the wear rate is reduced to 0.27 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 87.3%. Continuing to increase the load to 150 N, with pure water lubrication, the film wear rate was a minimum of 0.186 × 10−9 mm3/Nm, a reduction of 84.2%.
Overall, Cu-CQDs film is formed on the surface of the steel disc, its wear resistance is improved and the film plays a good protective role for the base material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology analysis of wear scars
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 shows the wear area of steel disc surface film and its surface morphology, wear morphology of steel ball surface, and its wear scar diameter lubricated with 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs solution at a load of 50 N. The wear area of pure steel disc was 535.8 μm2 under pure water lubrication, and the width of wear marks was larger, and the average wear scar diameter of steel ball was 334.0 μm. When 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs were added in the pure water, the wear area of pure steel disc was 176.2 μm2, and the average wear scar diameter was 326.2 μm.

For the three kinds of Cu-CQDs films formed on the surface of steel discs, all of the wear areas of steel discs and wear scar diameters of steel balls obviously reduced. The wear area of steel disc was 94.7 μm2 and wear scar diameter of steel ball was 277.4 μm for immersion 1 h lubricated with 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs solution. The surface wear of the steel disc is the smallest, the wear area is the smallest, 5.7 μm2, and the average wear scar diameter is 319.0 μm when the steel disc is lubricated with 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs solution and soaked for 2 h. From the visible lubricating film on the steel ball surface as shown in Table 3, it is clear that the Cu-CQDs or steel disc surface film component in the solution can easily contribute to the formation of a good lubricating film at the steel ball interface. The results could be used to clarify the friction coefficients and wear rates decreased lubricated with 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs solution.

Table 4 showed the wear area of the steel disc surface film and its surface morphology, wear morphology of steel ball surface, and its wear scar diameter lubricated with pure water at a load of 50 N. Under pure water lubrication, among the three films, the wear area of steel disc surface film was the smallest at 129.5 μm2 and the average wear scar diameter was the smallest at 220.0 μm when immersed for 1 h.

In conclusion, adding Cu-CQDs to pure water can significantly improve the wear resistance of the steel disc and friction reduction, meanwhile, the film formed on the surface of the steel disc has a good role in protecting the surface of the steel disc.

Table 5 shows the SEM analysis of worn discs and steel balls lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution. It can be seen that the surface of pure steel disc was very smooth without friction. The elements on the surface of pure steel disc are mainly carbon (4.24 wt%), cadmium (1.41 wt%), and iron (94.34 wt%), which belong to the components of GCr15 steel as shown in Table 6 .

The surface furrow of the steel disc was significantly reduced and the surface became smoother after lubrication with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution, and EDS analysis (in Table 6) indicates that Cu-CQDs are involved in the formation of boundary lubrication film and reduce wear to some extent. The copper content of tribo-film on the surface of steel disc was 29.38 wt%. The iron content of tribo-film was 65.09 wt%, decreased by 31% compared to water lubrication. These results prove the Cu-CQDs took part in the formation of tribo-film, resulting in low friction coefficient and wear rate. The SEM/EDS analysis were not conducted for pure steel ball and steel ball lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution because it has little effect on the experimental results.

The film formed on the surface of steel disc for immersion 0.5 h, and 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution as a lubricant, and there was a significant friction film formation in the film abrasion area on the surface of the steel disc, and the surface morphology after the SEM magnification could see those fine particles participated in the formation of the boundary lubrication film, indicating that the Cu-CQDs in pure water played a role in repairing the friction pair in time during the friction process. The EDS data also proves that there are the components of Cu-CQDs (5.81 wt% in Table 6) in the transfer film on the surface of the steel ball, so it plays an anti-wear and anti-friction effect.

The surface morphology and EDS analysis of the films (1 h immersion) rubbed with the addition of 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs in pure water. There are a lot of fine particles in the friction region of the thin film, mainly from Cu-CQDs in pure water, and the content of copper is up to 7.95 wt% in Table 6. The analysis of the components of the transfer film on the surface of the steel ball also showed that the content of copper element was as high as 30.32 wt%, which again indicated that the Cu-CQDs could repair the tribo-pair by the formation of transfer film, and protect the interface of the steel disc with the Cu-CQDs film, resulting in low friction coefficient and wear rate.

Under 0.5 wt % Cu-CQDs solution lubrication condition, obvious Cu-CQDs participate in the formation of friction film in the film area (in the inset image for 2 h immersion) on the surface of the steel disc. The copper element (4.63 wt% in Table 6) also exists on the surface of the steel ball, indicating that the friction-induced component transfer of the film or Cu-CQDs in water participate in the formation of the lubrication film, which plays an anti-wear and antifriction effect.

The surface morphology and EDS analysis of steel disc film after friction under pure water lubrication (film for immersion 1 h). Due to the prolonging of film growth time, the friction-induced film stripping is incomplete, and the copper element (1.71 wt% in Table 8) is detected on the surface of steel ball, which implies the formation of transfer film, so it plays an anti-wear and anti-friction effect.

For the film soaking for 2 h. The steel disc surface film is peeling under the action of water and friction, but the peeling is not complete. EDS analysis of the friction film revealed the continued presence of copper elements. The presence of high content of copper (3.56 wt% in Table 8) in the transfer film on the surface of the steel ball, indicated that the friction-induced film component forms a transfer film at the interface of the steel ball as the main reason for the anti-wear and friction reduction effect.

The tribological behavior mechanisms of Cu-CQDs and its films can be summarized as bellows: For Cu-CQDs, the “self-mending effect” and the Rehbinder effect play the important roles in reducing fiction and wear when it was used as an additive in pure water [29,40,41]. The particle size of Cu-CQDs in water is about 10 nm as shown in Fig. 2(a). Cu-CQDs particles are easy to enter the friction pair interface to play the role in mending surface during the friction process. The conclusion can be explained by measuring the friction pair interface roughness. The friction sub-interface roughness (0.829 μm, soaked for 1 h in Table 1) is larger than the particle agglomeration size (0.09 μm in Fig. 2 (b)), the agglomerate particles can also enter the interface, and resulting in reducing the friction coefficient. The “self-mending effect” could be expounded by friction induced the formation of lubrication films on the surface of steel discs and steel balls, preventing from direct contact between steel disc and steel ball, which is also one of the main reasons to reduce the friction coefficient and wear rate [42]. The previous SEM/EDS analysis results could be used to prove the conclusions. The copper contents of surface wear zones of steel discs and steel balls were obviously increased compared to pure water lubrication as shown in Table 6. The XPS results was also conducted to clarify the chemical valence of copper element (Cu2p spectrum in Fig. 11 (c)) on the surfaces of wear zones [43]. It is well known that the banding energy at 932.4 eV belongs to CuO (Cu2p3/2), and 952.9 eV belongs to CuO (Cu2p1/2). It is easy to find that the two peaks were detected on the surface of wear zones when 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs was added into pure water as a lubricant as shown in Fig. 11(c) (Lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs). The copper atom content of wear zones of steel disc is 4.24% in Table 9 , which can also prove that CQDs took part in forming the lubrication film to repair the tribo-pairs, which have better anti-wear and friction reduction effects.

For Cu-CQDs film, copper elements were also present in the steel ball interface by EDS analysis after lubrication with pure water or Cu-CQDs solution (in Table 6), implying that friction-induced the transfer film generation on the surface of balls, preventing from the direct contact between Cu-CQDs film and steel ball, including the copper element was a key factor in the reduction of friction coefficient and wear rate. In fact, the chemical valence of elements on the surface of steel balls were also measured. However, the wear zones of steel balls were too small to detect at the same testing conditions. So, the surface wear zones of steel discs were conducted to investigate the difference among the films in order to clarify the protection role of films. From Figs. 11(a), 283.7–284.4 eV belongs to C–C or (C–H), 287.5–288.4 belongs to CO. The relative intensity of the two peaks at the interface of the steel discs with films lubricated with pure water or 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution become weak, which indicated that the Cu-CQDs film protects the base material well. The similar results were also found in O1s spectrum of wear zones of steel discs with films lubricated with pure water or 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution. In general, the valence at 528.6–530.7 eV for O1s spectrum, which belongs to copper oxide (CuO or Cu2O), 530.3–531.4 eV belongs to copper hydroxide. It is obviously found the two peaks at 528.8 and 531.4 eV become weak (in Fig. 11(b)) that the surface oxidation behavior is lower and the copper oxides contents are significantly lower when Cu-CQDs film is present on the surface of steel discs, which also could be proved by the EDS analysis of oxygen contents of steel discs coated with the films as shown in Table 6. These results again show that the Cu-CQDs films protects the substrate material during the friction process, which reveals deposited film could be used as a lubrication coating.

In all, the Cu-CQDs in water could form the lubrication film and “self-mending effect” to protect the surface of steel discs and steel balls during friction process [44–46]. Besides, deposited Cu-CQDs film also can protect the base materials. At the same time, friction induced the formation of transfer films on the surface of mating part, resulting in the lower friction coefficient and wear rate. Besides, the composite lubrication film will form on the surface of tribo-pairs when deposited film of steel disc lubricated with CQDs solution. The presumed tribological mechanisms of CQDs and its films could be expounded in Fig. 12 .

In this paper, the Cu-CQDs and its films on the surface of steel discs were prepared successfully. The prepared Cu-CQDs and thin-film materials were systematically characterized and analyzed using a series of modern analytical tools. The tribological behavior of thin-film materials under lubrication with pure water and 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs solution was investigated using an UMT-2 tribometer. The specific results are as follows:
1)
The average size of Cu-CQDs particles is 10 nm. The particle size of about 90 nm when agglomerated in water, which was smaller than that of surface roughness (0.829 μm) of film on the surface of steel disc immersion for 1 h.
2)
Compared with pure water, when 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs is used as the lubricating medium, the CQDs particles and films could effectively protects the interface of the matrix material, and played an important role in modifying of friction and wear properties of bearing steel discs. The lower friction coefficient and wear rate of film-coated steel discs were achieved lubricated with 0.5 wt% Cu-CQDs.
3)
The tribological mechanism of Cu-CQDs were ascribed to the formation of lubrication film including the components of Cu-CQDs. The tribological mechanism of Cu-CQDs films was attributed to friction-induced formation of transfer film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express thanks to Mrs Yanjie Chen for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS and HRTEM images. The financial support received from National Nature Foundation of China (52075144) and Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (2008085ME167), Anhui University Outstanding Young Talents Programs (gxyqZD2020051), the Talent Research Fund of Hefei University (21-22RC33) and Graduate Innovation and Entrepreneurship Program (21YCXL37) and the Open Project of Anhui Province Engineering Laboratory of Intelligent Demolition Equipment (APELIDE2021B003) and Excellent Scientific Research and Innovation Team of Anhui University (2022AH010096) are gratefully acknowledged.
